governanceItems.label: Confirm the FIS/TSYS retains full ownership of all data at all times, including during and after transition.
Confirm no proprietary data formats will be used — all data exportable in standard formats at any time.
Confirm full data extraction rights upon contract exit, at no additional cost to the client.
Confirm you can provide a side-by-side coexistence environment for a minimum 12-month dual-run period.
Confirm you will fund technical debt remediation where your technology introduction creates new technical debt.
Confirm you will provide AI governance documentation covering bias monitoring, model drift, and explainability for all AI tools deployed.
Confirm AI overlays can be integrated via API without requiring migration of core telephony or processing platforms.
Confirm the client retains ownership of telephony infrastructure and IVR design unless explicitly agreed otherwise.
Confirm a full coexistence architecture plan will be provided prior to go-live, covering the full transition period.
Confirm all tooling (including AI Overlays) will be pre approved by the FIS/TSYS Governance team, comprised of architecture, AI Governance and Security Operation, prior to implementation.
Confirm you will provide quantified, contractually referenceable projections of AHT reduction, call deflection, and cost impact from AI overlays.

migrationNotes:
Part 1 — Wave Composition: For each wave, list which client cohorts are included, explaining what criteria drove the grouping; for all clients, specify which client is mapped to each wave.  FIS has intentionally not prescribed a wave structure or grouping methodology. We expect vendors to analyze the client dataset provided and propose the wave design they believe is most operationally sound for an engagement of this complexity. There is no correct number of waves. Responses that demonstrate genuine analytical thinking about client risk, regulatory complexity, volume sequencing, and transition readiness will be evaluated more favorably than generic frameworks.

Phased and Risk-Balanced Approach: By sequencing NA (Columbus, Canada, PHP) first, we adopt a phased execution strategy - 
•	Lower Regulatory and Complexity Risks, compared to Europe
•	Stabilize high-confidence regions early
•	Incorporate learnings and best practices from similar previous engagements
•	Genpact’s approach in Canada would be to rehire employees following a clear and compliant separation from FIS. 
•	This structured rehire model ensures full adherence to local labor and employment regulations while enabling continuity of services, retention of critical skills, and minimal disruption to ongoing operations
•	PHP - Rebadging in the Philippines ensures uninterrupted delivery by retaining experienced, client‑embedded talent in a proven, cost‑efficient location already supporting Gamma 7 operations

The UK is partially aligned through Wave 1, targeting segments with comparable readiness and risk profiles.
•	UK Wave 1 includes roles with minimal regulatory dependency and no requirement for extended consultation, allowing alignment with the NA timeline and early stabilization of critical operations.

13 clients serving from Canada region

3 clients serving from Milton Keynes

Risk: Consultation & Regulatory Delays - Extended employee consultation or regulatory requirements may delay rebadging timelines.
Mitigation:
•	Plan consultations early with defined milestones and owners.
•	Partner with local HR and Legal to meet all statutory obligations.
•	Reuse proven materials and playbooks from NA and UK Wave 1.

Risk: Employee Uncertainty & Attrition (Wave 2) - The execution of rebadging in Wave 1, combined with a time gap of 2 months before Wave 2, may create uncertainty and perceived inequity among remaining employees, reducing engagement and increasing the risk of attrition in critical roles.
Mitigation:
•	Deploy a focused change management and communications plan for Wave 2.
•	Provide clear, regular updates on timing, rationale, and next steps.
•	Identify critical roles and implement targeted retention or engagement actions.

Risk: Operational Disruption During Rebadging
Wave 2 roles may be operationally critical, increasing the risk of service disruption if transitions are poorly sequenced or executed.
Mitigation:
•	Complete role level criticality and dependency assessments ahead of execution.
•	Establish robust transition and knowledge transfer plans.
•	Sequence execution to avoid peak operational demand conflicts.

Risk: Regulatory and compliance gaps due to diverse labor laws, employment regulations, and consultation requirements across multiple European countries.
Mitigation:  Country-Specific Regulatory Assessment - Engage local legal expertise to validate regulatory interpretations and ensure alignment with jurisdiction‑specific requirements.
Identify countries requiring works council or employee representative consultation early in the planning phase.
Ensure documentation, contracting, and onboarding fully align with local employment regulations.

2 clients service from Hamburg region

Wave 1 (Columbus, Canada, PHP and Milton Keynes)

Populate a RACI of what TSYS/FIS will be responsible for and what you will be responsible for; FIS has not pre-populated this RACI. Vendors are expected to propose the full activity list they believe is required to execute a migration of this scale, and to assign responsibility accordingly. Responses should reflect your actual delivery methodology — not a generic RACI template. Activities should cover at minimum: client readiness, knowledge transfer, dual-run management, cutover approval, stabilization, and hypercare exit. Add rows as needed.

Project charter / flying formation and establish governance model

Define the communication calendar & key dates for the consultative process

HR due diligence / benefits harmonization / retention strategy

Complete as-is process documentation assessment and agree on the  approach to close the gaps

Future state control ownership and framework design (retained + delivery center)

Notification letters / offer letters release

Conduct training sessions for retained teams per change and comms. plan (new ways of working)

Complete process training for backfill and bench resources as per plan

evaluationBriefText:
GENPACT
Vendor Evaluation Brief
FIS TMS Managed Services RFP (TMS-RFP-2026-001)
March 30, 2026 | Confidential
Evaluation Tear Sheet
Total data points: 69
Executive Assessment Memo
GENPACT
Bottom Line
Genpact offers the most commercially aggressive proposal in the field — $325M in 5-year savings and a target end-state of just 891 FTE (67% reduction) by Year 7 — anchored by an existing FIS relationship and a proven 1,600+ FTE rebadge track record. However, the proposal requires a 7-year minimum commitment (not 5), carries a negative $28M Year 1 cost above baseline, and rests on a 68% committed productivity assumption that implies near-total automation of contact center operations. The “certainty commitment” on efficiency savings is marked “TBD” for every geography, and COLA is exclusive — meaning annual cost escalation sits on top of the quoted rates. This is a high-reward / high-complexity proposition that demands rigorous due diligence on the productivity assumptions and the true 7-year total cost of ownership.
Underlying Assumptions in their Proposal
7-year mandatory deal term: The minimum contract is 7 years, not 5. Quoted rates explicitly assume this duration (Assumption #22, Tab 6.0). The 5-year TCV of $425M appears competitive, but the 7-year lock-in is the longest in the vendor field. If FIS wants 5-year optionality, the pricing basis changes entirely.
68% committed productivity over 7 years: This implies reducing 2,678 FTE to 891 — a 67% headcount reduction. The efficiency levers are: 68% labor arbitrage (US&CA), 40% AI/digital (combined), with 90% of US/UK work offshored by Month 12. All AI tools run on Genpact AI Platform with Claude (Anthropic) and MCP architecture. No individual tool-level savings are committed (“TBD” in all K-column cells). (Tab 9.0, Tab 5.0!K50:K58)
COLA is exclusive: Unlike Cognizant which bundles COLA, Genpact charges COLA annually on top of rates (3-5% by country). Over 7 years, this compounds significantly and is not reflected in the headline savings figure. (Tab 6.0!C100, C41-C47)
50% WFH assumed across all locations: This is a material cost reduction lever (no facility costs for half the workforce) but creates PCI DSS compliance risk for voice operations handling cardholder data. No other vendor assumes 50% WFH. (Tab 6.0!C113)
Strengths & Differentiators
Existing FIS relationship with proven rebadge execution: Genpact has already rebadged 1,600+ FTE for FIS in the US with 98% offer acceptance. This is not hypothetical capability — it is demonstrated performance on this exact client. The GTM framework for growth and new product launches is already in place. (Proposal p.3, p.12)
Strongest tech governance in the field: 11 of 12 governance commitments at “Commit” (vs. Cognizant at 4/12). Commits to open APIs, FIS data ownership, 12-month coexistence, AI governance documentation, quantified AI projections, and pre-approval by FIS governance. Only “Partial” on tech debt remediation. (Tab 5.0!C32:C43)
NICE Platinum Partner with detailed migration methodology: 14 NAM waves + 9 EMEA waves with 12-month parallel runs and fallback readiness. This is the most granular CCaaS migration plan submitted, backed by Genpact’s deep CXone implementation capability. (Proposal p.14-16)
Gain-share alternative offered: Genpact proposes an option where FIS pays $0 upfront for transformation charges ($17.4M), with recovery through an 80/20 gain-share split declining to 95/5 by Year 5. This de-risks FIS’s investment while aligning incentives. (Proposal p.7)
Key Risks & Concerns
Year 1 cost is $28M ABOVE baseline: FIS will spend $172M in Y1 vs a $144M baseline. This includes $25M+ in one-time transition costs (setup, rebadge, tech overlay, training, professional services). The savings trajectory is extremely back-loaded: Y1 is negative, Y2 breaks even vs. target, and Y3+ delivers massive savings. FIS must fund a significant cash outlay before seeing returns. (Tab 6.0!C131:F131)
Certainty commitment is “TBD” for every geography: Despite claiming 68% productivity, the Tab 9.0 certainty column shows “TBD” for US&CA, NL, DE, UK, and Philippines. This means none of the efficiency assumptions are contractually committed yet. The AI tool benefits are also all marked “TBD”. This is the single largest gap between the headline savings figure and what FIS can contractually enforce. (Tab 9.0!H8:H12; Tab 5.0!K50:K58)
No references provided: All three client references say “to be provided later at the time of reference checks.” FIS cannot independently validate Genpact’s claims before the April 6 down-selection. The case studies reference a “Project Gamma 2” (FinTech processor) with 1,240 FTE rebadge and $23M cost-out, but no contactable reference is supplied. (Tab 3.0!D20:D22)
50% WFH assumption creates regulatory exposure: In a PCI DSS / FCA-regulated environment, 50% work-from-home for voice agents handling cardholder data is a significant compliance risk. No other vendor assumes this level of WFH. If FIS or end-clients require 100% WFO, the cost model changes materially. (Tab 6.0!C113)
Key Questions to Pressure-Test in Workshop
Your certainty commitment is “TBD” for every geography. When will this be finalized, and what contractual mechanism are you prepared to offer? If FIS requires at-risk pricing tied to the 68% productivity commitment, what changes in your commercial model?
Walk us through the Y1 cash flow: FIS pays $172M in Y1 against a $144M baseline. What is the earliest quarter in which FIS achieves cumulative break-even? Provide a quarter-by-quarter cumulative savings waterfall.
Your COLA is exclusive. Over 7 years at 3-5% per country, what is the total COLA impact on TCV? Provide the 7-year TCV inclusive of COLA at your stated rates.
50% WFH: Which specific functions and which client queues are you proposing for WFH delivery? How do you address PCI DSS compliance for voice agents handling cardholder data from home? What is the cost delta if FIS requires 100% WFO?
You have an existing FIS relationship. Describe how the transition from the current engagement scope to the full TMS scope will be governed. Are there any conflicts between the current SOW and this proposed engagement?
Go-Forward Implications
Genpact’s proposal reveals that the most aggressive cost structure requires a 7-year commitment and exclusive COLA — FIS should require all vendors to price both 5-year and 7-year scenarios with COLA-inclusive rates to enable a true apples-to-apples comparison.
The gain-share alternative ($0 upfront, 80/20 split) is a structurally different commercial model that FIS should evaluate independently. If two vendors offer gain-share, FIS gains significant optionality — paying only when savings materialize.
Genpact’s existing FIS relationship is a genuine de-risking factor, but also creates a potential single-vendor dependency. FIS should assess whether awarding TMS to an existing partner concentrates risk or creates operational synergies.

snippets.questionText: How many years has your organization delivered end-to-end risk mitigation covering fraud, collections, disputes, and credit bureau management. Report the number of years your organization has continuously operated this function. Do not sum individual staff years across people — report the longest unbroken period your organization has actively delivered this service.
snippets.text:
Genpact has been providing fraud prevention and dispute  operations solutions to various banking and capital markets clients over the last 25+ years . The solutions provided by Genpact cover the entire end to end fraud prevention lifecycle including fraud related inbound/outbound calls/chats (includes customer verification, transaction review, case intake, account actions) , detection alert review, alert investigation, and account actions.

Our dispute operations front operations capabilities include dispute intake through various channels including calls, email, and chat channels. This includes identifying the dispute category and creating cases for back-office operations.  In back-office operations we support dispute investigation, chargeback eligibility checks,  chargeback assessment,  documents review,  chargeback management from 1st chargeback to arbitration support for our clients. Our fraud recovery teams are experienced in reviewing and validating cases and managing recoveries. 
 We are a team of 10,000 + fraud and dispute management professionals, operating out of 10 centers across the globe.

COLLECTIONS:
Genpact brings over 25+ years of global collections experience, delivering measurable outcomes through digital, data, and AI‑led operations across 30+ countries and 52+ languages. Genpact supports end‑to‑end collections covering customer engagement, hardship handling, compliance, disputes, analytics, and escalations. 
Proven benchmarks include up to 20–30% efficiency gains, 15% improvement in cash collected, 5% reduction in complaints and  35-40% Self serve/ Containment. Genpact combines deep domain expertise, scalable cloud platforms, and agentic AI to optimize recovery, compliance, and customer experience.

snippets.questionText: Describe your organization's overall track record serving card-issuing financial institutions. Include years in market, number of active issuer clients, approximate ACV managed, and any notable performance milestones.
snippets.text: Genpact delivers end-to-end Customer Experience services to global clients across Banking & Financial Services, Fintech & Hi-Tech, Manufacturing, Life Sciences, Healthcare, CPG, and Insurance. We manage $130Bn+ in retail lending assets and support 600+ processes across the retail banking value chain, including Omni-Channel Customer Service, Contact Centre Analytics & Dashboards, Account Maintenance, Query & Dispute Management, Fraud, Payments, Billing and Refunds, Chargeback Processing, Ongoing AML Screening and Transaction Monitoring, KYC Refresh/EDD for High-Risk customers, and Service to Sales. Our operations span Omni-Channel Operations, Transformation Services, and Customer Experience Analytics, with proven delivery across prepaid, HCCA, and fraud authentication and verification services. Globally, we drive large-scale transformation for Tier 1 banks — achieving a 50%+ productivity improvement in the first four years of a five-year strategic partnership across Personal, Commercial, Business Banking, Mortgages, Payments, Collections, and Financial Crime. Our global footprint combines consulting, digital, technology, analytics, and intelligent operations, leveraging agentic AI, advanced ML models, GenAI, and scalable cloud infrastructure to deliver operational efficiency, enhanced customer experience, and digital modernization across industries, domains, and geographies. Notable performance milestones  include an Industry leading NPS score of 84 and $300+ million impact delivered.

snippets.questionText: How many years has your organization delivered back-office processing for card programs. Scope includes: imaging, correspondence processing, payment research, balance transfers, credit balance refunds, account maintenance, general ledger (GL) balancing and settlement, reconciliation of GL accounts, and returned mail processing. snippets.text:
Genpact has been delivering large scale back office processing for card programs for 25+ years, supporting Tier 1 global banks, card issuers and fintech’s across highly regulated environments.

Our end to end operations capabilities span onboarding & issuance, servicing & maintenance, payment processing, settlements, and reconciliations, underpinned by strong controls and compliance. Our back office capabilities cover the full breadth of card program support, including imaging and document digitization, correspondence processing, exception handling, account maintenance, GL processing and reconciliations, returned mail handling, and compliance driven reporting, delivered across hundreds of processes supporting billions in assets and transactions globally. 

We combine deep domain expertise with Agentic AI, GenAI enabled solutions, predictive models, and advanced workflow orchestration to drive straight through processing, reduce manual effort, and improve accuracy. This integrated model has delivered higher STP, significant AHT reduction, lower rework, and improved reconciliation auto match rates, enabling scalable, resilient, and cost efficient card operations.

snippets.questionText: For each asset, confirm acquisition intent and proposed transfer date. Where declining, state your transition assumptions.
snippets.text:
Target Date to be discussed and agreed upon mutually with client.

Genpact assumes that all required line‑of‑business applications, Office productivity software, and collaboration tools will be provided, supported, and appropriately licensed by FIS. We further assume that Genpact users will operate either on FIS standard image laptops, as in previous Gamma waves, or within an FIS‑provided VDI environment.

Target Date to be discussed and agreed upon mutually with client.

This decision depends on the endpoints’ acceptable operating condition, warranty coverage, and fair market value. We assume that the endpoints included in the asset transfer scope will have sufficient remaining useful life, be in good working condition, and be covered by an adequate warranty. The laptop transfer can be addressed at a later stage, consistent with the approach followed during previous Gamma waves once the asset inventory is shared and IT solution approach is finalized

Target Date to be discussed and agreed upon mutually with client.

As part of our proposal, we have assumed that FIS's network services will continue at the site. Networking component transfer is not considered in the current proposal, and we can explore with a proper DD, however, is not included in our standard proposal and any adaptation is a joint effort and decision as it impacts costs

Target Date to be discussed and agreed upon mutually with client.

As part of our proposal, we assume that FIS network services will continue to be available at the site. Any additional component transfers, including networking, are not included in the current proposal. Such transfers may be explored following appropriate due diligence; however, they fall outside the scope of our standard proposal. Any required adaptations would be subject to joint discussion and agreement, as they have cost implications.

snippets.questionText: Part 1 — Wave Composition: For each wave, list which client cohorts are included, explaining what criteria drove the grouping; for all clients, specify which client is mapped to each wave.  FIS has intentionally not prescribed a wave structure or grouping methodology. snippets.text:
40 Clients serving from Columbus region

Phased and Risk-Balanced Approach: By sequencing NA (Columbus, Canada, PHP) first, we adopt a phased execution strategy - 
•	Lower Regulatory and Complexity Risks, compared to Europe
•	Stabilize high-confidence regions early
•	Incorporate learnings and best practices from similar previous engagements
•	Genpact’s approach in Canada would be to rehire employees following a clear and compliant separation from FIS. 
•	This structured rehire model ensures full adherence to local labor and employment regulations while enabling continuity of services, retention of critical skills, and minimal disruption to ongoing operations
•	PHP - Rebadging in the Philippines ensures uninterrupted delivery by retaining experienced, client‑embedded talent in a proven, cost‑efficient location already supporting Gamma 7 operations

The UK is partially aligned through Wave 1, targeting segments with comparable readiness and risk profiles.
•	UK Wave 1 includes roles with minimal regulatory dependency and no requirement for extended consultation, allowing alignment with the NA timeline and early stabilization of critical operations.

Risk: Change communication and logistics  - Delayed/Inadequate communication due to multi country engagement
Mitigation:  
•	Drive region‑specific communication aligned with the Transition Plan, supported by a detailed change communication roadmap initiated 
•	Open lines of communication – multi level governance

Risk - Attrition and knowledge loss - Low offer acceptance rate / High rate of Offer drops 
Mitigation - 
•	Ring fence key resources early – use retention tools 
•	Proactive contingency hiring to manage any gaps

13 clients serving from Canada region

2 clients serving from PHP

3 clients serving from Milton Keynes

3 clients serving from Milton Keynes

Remaining UK (50%) deferred to Wave 2, which provides a longer runway to address regulatory and complexity‑led dependencies before cutover, reducing execution and compliance risk
•	UK Wave 2 may include roles subject to formal consultation requirements, increased regulatory dependency, or additional change‑management readiness.
•	Sequencing these roles in Wave 2 allows sufficient time to complete consultation processes and address employee feedback
•	Execution of Wave 2 will incorporate learnings from NA and UK Wave 1, enabling a more informed, compliant, and lower‑risk transition while maintaining business continuity.

Risk: Consultation & Regulatory Delays - Extended employee consultation or regulatory requirements may delay rebadging timelines.
Mitigation:
•	Plan consultations early with defined milestones and owners.
•	Partner with local HR and Legal to meet all statutory obligations.
•	Reuse proven materials and playbooks from NA and UK Wave 1.

Risk: Employee Uncertainty & Attrition (Wave 2) - The execution of rebadging in Wave 1, combined with a time gap of 2 months before Wave 2, may create uncertainty and perceived inequity among remaining employees, reducing engagement and increasing the risk of attrition in critical roles.
Mitigation:
•	Deploy a focused change management and communications plan for Wave 2.
•	Provide clear, regular updates on timing, rationale, and next steps.
•	Identify critical roles and implement targeted retention or engagement actions.

Risk: Operational Disruption During Rebadging
Wave 2 roles may be operationally critical, increasing the risk of service disruption if transitions are poorly sequenced or executed.
Mitigation:
•	Complete role level criticality and dependency assessments ahead of execution.
•	Establish robust transition and knowledge transfer plans.
•	Sequence execution to avoid peak operational demand conflicts.

snippets.text:
Screen capture controls: Screen printing is blocked, and screen capture is disabled for remote/WFH operations.

Device management and endpoint controls: Mobile access to corporate networks, data, applications, and systems follows documented policies, with Mobile Device Management and Data Leak Prevention controls. Endpoints use encrypted laptop drives, USB ports disabled by default (enabled only with business justification and approval), DLP monitoring for storage-media events, and mobile restrictions that block copy-paste and discourage saving data to local phone storage.

Network security and remote access controls: Remote access uses enhanced VPN, firewall controls, and DLP protections for WFH. Network entry points are protected by IDS/IPS and stateful firewalls, and access is allowed only from PCI-certified, authorized devices.

Print restrictions and data handling: Printing is restricted to protect data confidentiality. Only client VDI access is enabled from the endpoint, and client data is not expected to be stored in Genpact’s environment; access occurs within a client-managed VDI.

Cardholder-data protection: Card data in Genpact’s custody is encrypted per business, regulatory, and contractual requirements.
